terms.definition: The largest tarsal bone, forming the heel and articulating with the talus and cuboid.
terms.exampleSentence: Tenderness over the calcaneus on palpation suggested a stress fracture.
terms.contextNotes: Adjective form 'calcaneal' is used in most clinical phrases.

terms.definition: The tarsal bone that sits between the tibia/fibula and the calcaneus, forming the ankle joint.
terms.exampleSentence: An osteochondral lesion of the talus was demonstrated on MRI.
terms.contextNotes: Adjective 'talar'; vulnerable to avascular necrosis after fracture.

terms.definition: A boat-shaped tarsal bone on the medial midfoot between the talus and the cuneiforms.
terms.exampleSentence: A navicular stress fracture was suspected in the long-distance runner.
terms.contextNotes: An accessory navicular can mimic posterior tibial tendinopathy.

terms.definition: A cube-shaped tarsal bone on the lateral midfoot articulating with the calcaneus and metatarsals.
terms.exampleSentence: Cuboid syndrome was considered after the patient inverted her ankle.
terms.contextNotes: Often subluxes inferomedially after lateral ankle sprain.

terms.definition: Three wedge-shaped tarsal bones (medial, intermediate, lateral) articulating with the navicular and metatarsals.
terms.exampleSentence: Pain localised over the medial cuneiform raised suspicion of a Lisfranc injury.
terms.contextNotes: Three bones: medial, intermediate, lateral.

terms.definition: The five long bones of the midfoot connecting the tarsus to the phalanges, numbered one to five.
terms.exampleSentence: A fracture of the fifth metatarsal was confirmed on the oblique view.
terms.contextNotes: Number from medial (1st) to lateral (5th).

terms.definition: The bones of the toes; two in the hallux and three in each of the lesser toes.
terms.exampleSentence: Fractures of the proximal phalanges were managed with buddy strapping.
terms.contextNotes: Singular: phalanx; plural: phalanges.

terms.definition: The great toe; the first digit of the foot containing two phalanges.
terms.exampleSentence: The hallux was deviated laterally, consistent with hallux valgus.
terms.contextNotes: Use 'hallux' in clinical writing; 'big toe' in patient-facing speech.

terms.definition: A thick fibrous band running from the calcaneus to the metatarsal heads, supporting the longitudinal arch.
terms.exampleSentence: The plantar fascia was tender at its calcaneal insertion.
terms.contextNotes: Inflammation of this structure causes plantar fasciitis.

terms.definition: The strong tendon formed by gastrocnemius and soleus, inserting into the posterior calcaneus.
terms.exampleSentence: A palpable gap in the Achilles tendon and a positive Thompson test confirmed the rupture.
terms.contextNotes: Always capitalise 'Achilles'.

terms.definition: Tendon of tibialis posterior passing behind the medial malleolus to insert mainly on the navicular.
terms.exampleSentence: Posterior tibial tendon dysfunction was the likely cause of the acquired flat foot.
terms.contextNotes: Dysfunction is the commonest cause of adult-acquired flatfoot.

terms.definition: Tendons of peroneus longus and brevis passing behind the lateral malleolus, everting the foot.
terms.exampleSentence: Peroneal tendon subluxation was suggested by snapping behind the lateral malleolus.
terms.contextNotes: Modern anatomical term is 'fibularis'; both used in practice.

terms.definition: The lengthwise arch of the foot, divided into medial and lateral components, supported by ligaments and muscles.
terms.exampleSentence: The medial longitudinal arch collapsed on weight-bearing.
terms.contextNotes: Loss of this arch defines pes planus.

terms.definition: The side-to-side arch of the foot at the level of the metatarsal bases and cuneiforms.
terms.exampleSentence: Loss of the transverse arch contributed to the splayed forefoot.
terms.contextNotes: Collapse causes central metatarsalgia and callus under MT heads.

terms.definition: Metatarsophalangeal joint; the joint between a metatarsal head and the proximal phalanx of a toe.
terms.exampleSentence: Pain and swelling of the first MTPJ raised suspicion of acute gout.
terms.contextNotes: First MTPJ is the classic site for gout and hallux valgus.

terms.definition: The joint between the talus and calcaneus permitting inversion and eversion of the hindfoot.
terms.exampleSentence: Subtalar joint stiffness limited inversion to 10 degrees.
terms.contextNotes: Inversion/eversion happens here, not at the ankle mortise.

terms.definition: The bracket-shaped socket formed by the distal tibia, fibula and malleoli that articulates with the talus.
terms.exampleSentence: Widening of the ankle mortise on the AP view indicated a syndesmotic injury.
terms.contextNotes: A 'mortise view' is a 15–20° internally rotated AP radiograph.

terms.definition: The upper, non-weight-bearing surface of the foot opposite the sole.
terms.exampleSentence: Erythema and swelling were noted across the dorsum of the foot.
terms.contextNotes: Adjective 'dorsal'; opposite is plantar.

terms.definition: The sole of the foot; the weight-bearing under-surface.
terms.exampleSentence: A 2-cm ulcer was present on the plantar surface beneath the first metatarsal head.
terms.contextNotes: Use 'plantar' in clinical writing; 'sole' with patients.

terms.definition: The bony prominence on the outer side of the ankle, formed by the distal end of the fibula.
terms.exampleSentence: Bony tenderness over the lateral malleolus met the Ottawa ankle criteria for radiography.
terms.contextNotes: Distal fibula; injured in most lateral ankle sprains.

terms.definition: The bony prominence on the inner ankle formed by the distal end of the tibia.
terms.exampleSentence: Tenderness over the medial malleolus prompted ankle radiographs.
terms.contextNotes: Posterior tibial tendon runs just posterior to it.

terms.definition: Two small bones embedded within the flexor hallucis brevis tendon beneath the first metatarsal head.
terms.exampleSentence: Localised tenderness under the first metatarsal head suggested sesamoiditis.
terms.contextNotes: Bipartite sesamoid is a normal variant, not a fracture.

terms.definition: The vascular soft tissue beneath the nail plate to which the plate adheres.
terms.exampleSentence: Subungual haematoma involving more than 50% of the nail bed warranted trephination.
terms.contextNotes: Damage to the nail bed (not matrix) usually permits normal regrowth.

terms.definition: The germinal tissue beneath the proximal nail fold from which the nail plate grows.
terms.exampleSentence: Permanent nail ablation requires destruction of the nail matrix.
terms.contextNotes: Phenol or surgical excision used in partial nail avulsion procedures.

terms.definition: The thickened layer of skin (cuticle) overlying the proximal nail fold and protecting the matrix.
terms.exampleSentence: Inflammation of the eponychium was consistent with acute paronychia.
terms.contextNotes: Use 'cuticle' when speaking with patients.

terms.definition: The skin under the free edge of the nail plate forming a seal at the fingertip or toe tip.
terms.exampleSentence: Debris collected beneath the hyponychium in the onychomycotic nail.
terms.contextNotes: Distal counterpart of the eponychium; often hyperkeratotic in fungal nail.

terms.definition: The seven bones of the hindfoot and midfoot: talus, calcaneus, navicular, cuboid and three cuneiforms.
terms.exampleSentence: Stress fractures of the tarsal bones are commoner in long-distance runners.
terms.contextNotes: Seven bones in total; do not confuse with metatarsals.

terms.definition: Anterior leg muscle whose tendon extends the great toe and assists ankle dorsiflexion.
terms.exampleSentence: Weakness of extensor hallucis longus suggested L5 radiculopathy.
terms.contextNotes: EHL weakness is a key motor sign of an L5 radiculopathy.

terms.definition: A strong triangular ligament on the medial ankle resisting eversion, fanning out from the medial malleolus.
terms.exampleSentence: Tenderness over the deltoid ligament suggested a high-energy eversion injury.
terms.contextNotes: Injured in eversion; less common than lateral ligament sprains.

terms.definition: The tarsometatarsal joint complex linking the midfoot to the forefoot, stabilised by the Lisfranc ligament.
terms.exampleSentence: A subtle gap between the first and second metatarsal bases suggested a Lisfranc injury.
terms.contextNotes: Easily missed; weight-bearing radiographs improve detection.
